narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 44% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they aim to admit students with strong odds of passing. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, well below the typical student's 5.7; a Year 3 decile of 1, even lower than the average 5.5; an LSAT score of 33 compared to the usual 36.7; an undergraduate GPA of 2.7 versus the average 3.21; middle family income; and you're a full-time White male applicant, which aligns closely with the majority of students in the dataset. The model weighed these details against patterns from thousands of past students to make its call. The most influential factor by far was your LSAT score, which nudged the prediction toward failure because it's notably below what succeeds for most. Right behind that was your Year 3 decile ranking, similarly pushing toward a fail outlook since it's in the bottom tier compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile came next in importance, again contributing to the riskier prediction due to its low standing relative to averages. Family income level followed, with your middle bracket slightly tilting things toward failure in the model's eyes. Your undergraduate GPA also played a role, as its below-average mark added to the concern about bar success. Less impactful but still noted were your racial background and full-time status, both of which had minimal sway but leaned slightly toward the failure side, while gender mattered the least. Overall, your academic metrics—especially LSAT and deciles—stood out as weaker than typical successful students, outweighing your more average demographics. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on realistically improvable areas like retaking the LSAT for a higher score, building stronger study habits to lift future GPAs or rankings, or seeking targeted prep programs that address low early performance patterns. With dedication to these, you could shift your profile closer to proven passers and reapply stronger next time. Keep pushing—you have the potential to turn this around.
